preFix.title: Whittled birch darning mushroom
preFix.subtitle: A functional darning mushroom in seasoned birch for mending socks and gloves
preFix.excerpt: A darning mushroom turned or whittled from a short birch branch stub. The domed cap holds the fabric taut for darning; the handle gives leverage when pulling the mushroom out. Birch is traditional and durable.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the small-treen turning and whittling method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the darning-mushroom household tool form.

content.text: A darning mushroom is a smooth wooden dome on a short handle. The fabric to be darned goes over the dome and is held taut against it; the needle passes through the fabric without the dome shifting. The form is simple but the proportions matter: too small a dome and the fabric puckers; too large and the handle is lost inside a sock heel.
Seasoned
birch gives a smooth dense surface that the fabric slides over cleanly and that polishes with use.
Wood
Birch is the traditional species and the recommendation: it polishes smooth, accepts the Danish-oil surface well, and is available in short off-cuts from any hardwood supplier. The
blank

needs to be 90 mm long and 55 mm in diameter. A short section of birch branch or a dowel stub both work. Beech is a close alternative: denser, takes an even finer polish, and is the species most often used in manufactured darning mushrooms.
Tools
Sloyd knife: for all shaping.
Sandpaper, 320 grit: the dome surface must be very smooth so the fabric slides freely; sanding to 320-grit is not optional here.
Leather strop: strop every fifteen minutes.
Danish oil: builds a hard surface film that keeps the dome smooth with use.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Shaping the dome
The dome is 50 mm in diameter and 25 mm tall. Mark the dome profile on the side of the blank: it is a semicircle at its widest and tapers to the underside edge. Use
push cuts

from the top centre toward the rim edge to establish the curve. Work around the blank in quarters, taking equal amounts from each quarter, then work again in eighths between the quarter lines. The dome must be symmetrical; an off-centre mushroom wobbles when the fabric holds it under the needle.
The handle

The handle is 40 mm long and 20 mm in diameter where it meets the dome, tapering to 15 mm at the base. Mark the shoulder line where the dome meets the handle. Use push cuts along the handle sides, working from the shoulder toward the base. The transition from dome to handle needs a smooth concave curve; a sharp shoulder would crack the mushroom if dropped on a hard floor.
Sanding

Sand the dome surface with 320-grit, working in the direction of the grain. The dome needs no tool-mark facets; the smooth surface is the functional requirement. Sand until no facets catch the light. The handle can be left with light knife-finish marks; only the dome needs to be sanded smooth.
Finishing

Apply one thin coat of Danish oil with a soft cloth, wipe off after ten minutes, and allow four hours to dry before a second coat. Two coats build a satin surface that is hard enough to resist fabric abrasion.
Variations
Darning egg: a full egg form (75 mm long, 45 mm wide) on a short handle for gloves and smaller items.
Beech version: the denser grain polishes to a finer surface and is more durable with heavy use.
Flat-bottomed version: leave the base of the dome flat rather than curved for setting on a table; some darners prefer it stable while threading the needle.
Care
Wipe with a dry cloth after use. If the surface dulls or roughens with age, lightly re-sand with 320-grit and apply a single coat of Danish oil.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Take three to four light push cuts from the edge of the flat area inward toward the top, rotating the blank between cuts. The flat spot should blend out over four to six passes.
attrs.symptom: The dome has a flat spot at the top

attrs.fix: Sand again with 320-grit under direct raking light to find remaining facets. Each unblended facet shows as a shadow on the dome surface.
attrs.symptom: The fabric catches on the dome surface

attrs.fix: Maintain 15 mm minimum diameter on the handle. If already thinner, wrap the handle with a few turns of cotton tape fixed with a dab of beeswax to restore grip.
attrs.symptom: The handle is too thin to grip through a sock
